mission: To bring soda back to the table as something joyful, nostalgic, and genuinely supportive of daily gut health.
vision: A fridge culture where the most wanted can is also the one that helps you feel good tomorrow.
positioning: Olipop is the grown-up soda for people who still crave root beer, cream soda, and orange fizz—but no longer want a sugar crash as the price of admission.
purpose: Olipop exists to reconcile two ideas the beverage aisle has kept apart for too long: pleasure and care. It makes wellness feel less like a chore and indulgence feel less like a compromise.
manifesto: We believe soda never needed to disappear. It needed a better script: less syrup, more substance, same cold-can thrill.
We are not here to make health look stern. We are here for fizz on a Tuesday, root beer with dinner, cream soda in the sun, and a gut that gets considered without becoming the whole personality.
Olipop is nostalgia with a nutrition label that can look you in the eye. Bright, fizzy, familiar—and quietly more intelligent than the sodas we grew up on.
identityIntro: The Olipop identity should feel like a vintage soda fountain seen through a clean modern lens: rounded, buoyant, colorful, and never clinical. Its world is built from soft cream grounds, deep navy lettering, fruit-parlor color, and the visual pleasure of bubbles rising.

Against a category drifting into probiotic sameness and wellness minimalism, Olipop owns optimistic abundance. It is credible because it is transparent, but loved because it remembers that soda is supposed to be fun.

world.title: Fizz
world.body: Fizz is the brand’s kinetic signature: bubbles, dots, arcs, and buoyant spacing that make every layout feel freshly opened. Motion should rise, pop, and settle like carbonation in a glass rather than slide with tech-brand smoothness.

world.title: Parlor
world.body: Olipop borrows from soda fountains, diners, and drugstore coolers, but avoids costume nostalgia. The cues are edited: rounded typography, cream backgrounds, striped details, and flavor colors that feel remembered rather than replicated.

world.title: Fiber
world.body: The health story is present, not preachy: prebiotics and fiber are handled with diagrammatic clarity and calm confidence. Claims should feel like a well-labeled pantry, not a supplement bottle shouting from a shelf.

world.title: Picnic
world.body: Olipop lives in casual social rituals: cans in hands, condensation on picnic tables, fruit on pale wood, sunlight through a kitchen window. The brand should always feel invited into real life, not staged on a wellness pedestal.

statement: Olipop is soda with a memory and a method. It keeps the flavors people have loved for decades, then rebuilds the recipe for bodies that have moved on from 40 grams of sugar.
The brand should never apologize for being soda. Its confidence comes from saying: yes, this is indulgent; yes, it is considered; yes, you can have both.

beliefs.title: Pleasure is not the enemy of progress.
beliefs.body: Olipop rejects the old bargain that better-for-you must mean less delicious. The brand treats flavor as the gateway to habit change, because people repeat what they actually crave.

beliefs.title: Nostalgia deserves an upgrade.
beliefs.body: Root beer, cream soda, grape, and cola are cultural comfort objects, not guilty relics. Olipop preserves their emotional architecture while changing the nutritional foundation underneath.

beliefs.title: Wellness should have bubbles.
beliefs.body: Gut health does not need to arrive dressed in beige restraint or medicinal seriousness. Olipop makes functional benefit feel social, colorful, and easy to reach for at lunch, on a picnic, or straight from the fridge.

quotes: The soda you remember, rebuilt for the body you live in.
Health food can have a pull tab.

concept: A rounded wordmark that behaves like a bubble finding its shape: buoyant letters, soft joins, and a lightly compressed rhythm that feels pulled from a vintage soda cooler, not a supplement aisle.
The double “o” becomes the quiet signature — two effervescent loops that suggest fizz, flavor, and the gut’s circular ecosystem without drawing a literal stomach.
Intent: make health feel craveable; the logo should pour, not prescribe.
logo.colorNote: The logo lives primarily in Sarsaparilla Ink on Cream Soda, with flavor-led color fields used as joyful interruptions. It should feel like a heritage soda mark cleaned up by modern nutrition science: familiar at first sip, smarter by the second.

variations.name: Primary Wordmark
variations.use: Use on cans, cartons, website headers, and any moment where Olipop needs full recognition and shelf confidence.

variations.name: Stacked Bubble Lockup
variations.use: Use for tight social avatars, merch patches, fridge stickers, and circular packaging seals where the double-o character can lead.

variations.name: One-Color Stamp
variations.use: Use in Sarsaparilla Ink or Cream Soda for shipping boxes, embossed lids, glassware, and low-ink production where nostalgia should feel tactile.

variations.name: Flavor Field Wordmark
variations.use: Use the full wordmark knocked out of single flavor colors for campaigns, limited drops, and high-energy retail signage.

safety: Keep clear space equal to the width of one “o” around all sides of the mark.
Use the mark large enough for the inner counter-shapes to stay open and fizzy.
Place on calm color fields; let photography and fruit props orbit the logo, never crowd it.
donts: Never add leaves, medical crosses, intestines, or probiotic clichés to the mark.
Never stretch, arch, or wobble the wordmark into faux-retro novelty.
Never use gradients inside the logo; the fizz comes from shape, not effects.
Never place the logo over busy fruit piles, condensation glare, or patterned picnic cloths.
Never set the logo in black when Sarsaparilla Ink can do the job with more appetite.
Never make the logo feel clinical, pharmaceutical, or worthy in a beige-wellness way.

philosophy: Olipop’s color world is a soda fountain seen through a wellness lens: creamy counters, dark syrup labels, bright fruit glass, and a green note that signals the prebiotic intelligence underneath. We are not chasing the neon hyperactivity of energy drinks or the pale restraint of functional beverages; this palette lets indulgence and digestion sit at the same table.

The system should feel collectible by flavor but unmistakably Olipop as a family. Cream grounds the optimism, ink gives it adult credibility, and the accent colors arrive like pours of strawberry, citrus, root beer, and garden freshness — nostalgic, but never dusty.

palette.name: Cream Soda
palette.meaning: A soft vanilla canvas that makes every flavor feel delicious before the can is even opened.

palette.name: Sarsaparilla Ink
palette.meaning: Deep blue-black with root-beer gravity; it gives the playfulness a grown-up spine.

palette.name: Prebiotic Sprout
palette.meaning: Fresh, botanical, and quietly functional — the signal that this soda is doing something good.

palette.name: Strawberry Glass
palette.meaning: Sweet without being sugary; a rosy flash of soda-shop pleasure.

palette.name: Tangerine Fizz
palette.meaning: A citrus spark that brings movement, carbonation, and sunny shelf pop.

palette.name: Root Beer Velvet
palette.meaning: A syrupy brown with vintage warmth, carrying the memory of classic soda into a cleaner future.

hierarchy.title: STRUCTURE
hierarchy.body: Use Cream Soda as the dominant field and Sarsaparilla Ink for the wordmark, claims, nutrition cues, and navigation. This pairing is the brand’s editorial voice: calm enough for wellness information, rich enough to still feel like soda.

hierarchy.title: ABUNDANCE
hierarchy.sub: Strawberry Glass + Tangerine Fizz + Root Beer Velvet
hierarchy.body: Use the flavor colors generously in blocks, bands, can families, and campaign crops so the brand feels like a stocked fridge rather than a single SKU. Each color should be poured in clean, confident areas; avoid confetti scatter, which makes the system feel childish instead of collectible.

hierarchy.title: HIGHLIGHT
hierarchy.body: Use Prebiotic Sprout as the functional wink: callouts, icons, fiber notes, gut-health moments, and small flashes in layouts. It should never dominate the brand, because Olipop’s point of view is that wellness is the benefit inside the treat, not the costume worn on top.

philosophy: Olipop type should feel like a soda-shop sign discovered in the future: round, fizzy, a little theatrical, but never childish. The system borrows the confidence of vintage beverage lettering and cleans it through a wellness lens, so indulgence reads as intentional rather than guilty.
Headlines carry the flavor hit; text carries the science. Big words can be bubbly and nostalgic, while ingredients, fiber claims, and functional benefits stay calm, crisp, and easy to trust.

fonts.name: Fizz Serif
fonts.note: Fraunces has the soft swelling curves of old soda typography with enough editorial polish to feel like a modern functional drink, not a novelty throwback.

fonts.name: Daily Gut Sans
fonts.note: Nunito Sans is rounded without becoming infantile, giving prebiotic facts a friendly, highly legible voice that matches the product’s optimistic ease.

scale.name: H1
scale.use: Campaign headlines, hero flavor moments, large can-side messages.

scale.name: H2
scale.use: Section titles, flavor families, retail headers, website modules.

scale.name: H3
scale.use: Functional callouts, benefit labels, limited-edition drops, shelf talkers.

scale.name: Body
scale.use: Ingredient notes, gut-health education, product descriptions, legal-adjacent copy.

rules: Set Fraunces large enough for its curves to feel carbonated; avoid using it below 28px.
Use Nunito Sans for every claim involving fiber, prebiotics, calories, or ingredients; credibility lives in restraint.
Headlines may stack tightly like vintage crate lettering, but never warp, arc, or fake distress.
Use uppercase sans labels with generous tracking for scientific clarity and shelf-speed scanning.
Pair one expressive line with one factual line: desire first, proof immediately after.
Never use script fonts, diner pastiche, or faux-1950s effects; nostalgia is an ingredient, not a costume.

inUse.title: ROOT BEER, REWRITTEN
inUse.body: Fraunces lands the emotional promise in one oversized, creamy line. Beneath it, Nunito Sans states the modern trade: 9g fiber, prebiotics, and the familiar snap of a classic soda ritual.

inUse.title: THE CAN THAT CUTS BOTH WAYS
inUse.body: A compact Fraunces headline frames Olipop as indulgent and functional at once. The supporting sans copy should be short enough to read in the aisle, like a wink followed by a receipt.

inUse.title: STRAWBERRY VANILLA IN THE SUN
inUse.body: Use generous display type over a pale cream or flavor-matched field, letting the can stay hero. Keep benefit language in a small uppercase band so the post feels appetizing before it feels educational.

inUse.title: PREBIOTIC SODA, NOT A COMPROMISE
inUse.body: The type hierarchy should refuse the category’s apology language. Fraunces says soda with pleasure; Nunito Sans explains why the body can come along for the ride.

intentIntro: Olipop’s creative intent is to stand apart from both legacy soda and functional beverage minimalism. It should feel more delicious than wellness, more trustworthy than soda, and more culturally warm than the prebiotic set.

patternStory.title: The Bubble Botanica
patternStory.body: Olipop’s pattern language is built from the two things most soda brands keep separate: fizz and flora. Tiny carbonation dots, fruit-slice moons, root curls, vanilla stars, and prebiotic fiber loops drift together like a microscopic garden seen through a soda glass. It gives the brand an ownable world where gut health feels lively, delicious, and visible without resorting to clinical diagrams.

patternSystem: Scale: use micro-patterns at 6–12px for texture and oversized motifs at 80–160px for flavor campaigns; avoid middle-ground wallpaper.
Opacity: keep background patterning at 8–18% on cream, or 22–35% when tone-on-tone with flavor colors.
Cropping: let fruit moons and bubble clusters run off edges, as if the can is too fizzy to stay inside the frame.
Layering: patterns may sit behind cans, inside headline panels, or as flavor-coded borders; never place over nutrition facts or functional claims.
When to avoid: skip patterning on dense retail shelves, ingredient panels, medical explainers, or anywhere the brand needs a single clean proof point.

philosophy: Olipop photography should look like the soda you wanted as a kid, served in the life you are trying to build now. It is sunlit, colorful, casually composed, and grounded in real drinking occasions rather than sterile wellness rituals.

The can is always the social spark: on a picnic blanket, next to melting ice, beside citrus peels, in a hand reaching across a table. The image should make gut health feel like part of the afternoon, not a separate appointment.

Against competitors leaning either candy-loud or pharmacy-clean, Olipop owns the optimistic middle: creamy backgrounds, honest ingredients, visible condensation, and a little retro wink. Every shot should say refreshment first, function second, credibility always.

inUse.title: FRAMING
inUse.body: Frame the can slightly forward of the scene, even in lifestyle compositions, so it feels like the reason the gathering started. Use eye-level or gentle three-quarter angles for friendliness; save dramatic low angles for launch campaigns only.

inUse.title: CROP
inUse.body: Crop into condensation, pulled tabs, poured foam, fruit slices, and the curve of the label. Close crops should feel tactile and appetite-led, giving the viewer sensory evidence before they read a benefit.

inUse.title: LIGHT
inUse.body: Use diffused daylight or studio light that mimics a kitchen window at 3pm. Shadows can be present but never harsh; the mood is bright enough for soda, warm enough for nostalgia.

inUse.title: PROPS
inUse.body: Use strawberries with stems, orange peels, vanilla flecks, root beer foam, striped straws, picnic textiles, and imperfect ice. Avoid lab glassware, yoga clichés, supplement scoops, or anything that makes pleasure feel supervised.

intent: Use color as flavor memory: cream soda softness, root beer brown, orange-pop brightness, strawberry pink, and fresh green as the quiet signal of gut support. Anchor everything in soft cream and dark navy so the world stays editorial, not candy-coated.

Design with rounded confidence: generous curves, clear claims, tactile photography, and playful carbonation details. The result is a brand that feels like opening the fridge at your happiest friend’s house—familiar, bright, and a little smarter than expected.
